Organic Chemistry I Lectures CHEM-C 341, Fall 2020
Week 7 Lecture Slides – Week of 10/5/2020
Instructor: Kasey J. Clear

More on Brønsted -Lowry definition
Measure acid strength using the acid-dissociation constant, K a
Higher K a values mean that a species more readily gives up its proton to water and is therefore a stronger acid

pH vs. pK a
pH is a measure of the concentration of H 3 O + in water
pK a is a measure of acidity (i.e. how easily an acid loses its proton to water or another proton acceptor)
pK a values of acids & organic functional groups

Acid-base reactions are part of our daily lives
Food, pharmaceuticals, lab
“Basics” of acids and bases
Definitions: Arrhenius, Brønsted , Lewis & pH
Acid strength: K a and pK a
pH and ionization state
Trends in acid strength ( pK a )
Effects of the atom attached to the proton: size, electronegativity, hybridization
Effects of other structural features: delocalization, nearby electronegative groups

pK a values of acids & organic functional groups

Factors affecting acid strength
Stability of the conjugate base
Bond strength of A-H bond
Solvent
What is the difference between an acid and its conjugate base?
What happens to acid strength ( pK a ) if we stabilize the conjugate base?
What happens to acid strength ( pK a ) if we destabilize the conjugate base?

Structural factors affecting acid strength: A-H
1.  Electronegativity (across the periodic table)
When a proton is attached to a more electronegative atom, it is more acidic
2.  Size (down the periodic table)
When a proton is attached to a larger atom, it is more acidic

Structural factors affecting acid strength: A-H
3.  Hybridization
When a proton is attached to a hybrid orbital with more s-character, it is 	more acidic

Structural factors affecting acid strength: nearby effects
1.  Delocalized electrons (a.k.a. resonance)
Species with resonance-stabilized conjugate bases are more acidic

Whenever there are delocalized electrons , charges and electrons are spread out across multiple atoms you can draw resonance structures to represent the different arrangements of electrons and charges
Delocalized electrons are possible when you have a series of 3 or more sp 2 or sp hybridized atoms
Delocalized Electrons

Delocalized Electrons
Rules for drawing resonance structures
Only move electrons!
Only lone pairs AND electrons in double and triple bonds ( π electrons) can move
The total number of electrons does not change and neither does the net charge
Examples with common organic functional groups?
Not all delocalized systems are created equally

Structural factors affecting acid strength: nearby effects
2.  Inductive effect
Nearby electronegative atoms can stabilize negative charge in the conjugate base

EWG stabilize negative charge in a conjugate base and increase acidity
examples of electron withdrawing groups:
-nitro
-carbonyl
-halogen
Electron withdrawing groups (EWG)

EDG destabilize negative charge in a conjugate base and decrease acidity
examples of electron donating groups:
-ethers
-alkyl groups
Electron donating groups (EDG)

Practice
Which is a stronger acid? H 2 S  vs.  H 2 O HI vs. HCl
Which is a stronger base?
H 2 O vs. NH 3
Which has a lower pK a ?
CH 3 CH 2 OH vs. CH 2 =CHOH
CH 3 CH 2 OH vs. CF 3 CH 2 OH

Organic chemists tend to refer to pK a only: base strength (basicity) is measured by the acidity of its conjugate acid
As basicity increases, the acidity of the conjugate acid decreases
Nitrogen is special because it acts as a base and can become cationic
What about bases?

Aliphatic nitrogen pK a ~ 10
Aromatic nitrogen pK a ~ 5
Strength of nitrogen bases (smaller pK a = weaker base)

Amide nitrogen pK a ~ 0
Guanidine nitrogen pK a >13
Strength of nitrogen bases (smaller pK a = weaker base)
